topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen glowed, a harsh, unforgiving light reflecting off the title: "The Unseen Architect." My breath hitched, a sharp, painful intake of air that burned in my chest. It wasn't mine. The byline, the entire body of work, was attributed to Julian Vance. My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them steady. I reread the introductory paragraph, the words blurring into a mocking tapestry of my own ideas, my own painstaking research. A cold, hollow feeling spread through my stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. I pushed back from the chair, the scrape of the wood against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. I needed to call someone, anyone, but the phone felt impossibly heavy, glued to my palm. Every nerve ending felt exposed, screaming a silent alarm.

[story 2]

I stared at the printout, the ink seeming to mock me with its polished professionalism. The name on the front page—Eleanor Hayes—was splashed across the page where my prose should have been. A wave of nausea rolled over me, so intense I had to lean against the cool plaster of the wall, pressing my forehead against it. My vision tunneled. How could this happen? I frantically scrolled through the digital archives, searching for any trace of my contribution, any digital fingerprint I’d left behind. Silence. Nothing. A frantic energy began to vibrate beneath my skin, a restless churning that made my jaw ache. I started pacing the small room, my footsteps echoing unevenly, a frantic rhythm against the silence. My throat tightened, making swallowing a physical ordeal.

[story 3]

When the editor’s email finally landed, the carefully constructed composure I’d maintained shattered instantly. The font of the reply was sterile, devoid of warmth, and the content was a curt dismissal. I crumpled the paper in my fist, the sound of the tearing paper loud and violent. I sank onto the floor, the rough carpet scratching against my knees. My vision swam, colors bleeding at the edges. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the article out, but it was trapped behind a thick, suffocating blanket of disbelief and betrayal. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound of pure, trapped frustration. I clawed at my own arms, feeling the frantic pulse hammering against my skin, a relentless, urgent drumbeat demanding release.
